The 5 Best Websites for Historical Financial Data
=================================================

Last Updated: May 19, 2025

![Image 3: Author](https://img.stockanalysis.com/cd35b0bc-8afb-4475-b42c-c6aebc4cd283/lincoln-olson.jpeg)

[Lincoln Olson](https://stockanalysis.com/contributor/lincoln-olson/)

Investor

Reviewed by [Mike Nkansah, MBA](https://stockanalysis.com/contributor/mike-nkansah/)

We mention products and services that we think can be helpful for our users. Some or all of them may be from partners who compensate us. This can influence which topics we choose and how products are presented on the page, but it does not affect our opinions or conclusions.

Real-time financial data is everywhere, but tracking down historical data for publicly traded companies can be more challenging.

This is especially true if you're trying to export a large dataset or, at the other end of the spectrum, trying to track down one very specific number or detail.

That's why I made this list.

You can use the summary section below to pinpoint the website most likely to have the data you're looking for, and then read more about the website further down.

Here are the five best websites and tools for accessing historical financial data.

Summary of the best sites for historical financial data
-------------------------------------------------------

**Website****Best for**

1. Stock Analysis: best overall
-------------------------------

[Stock Analysis](https://stockanalysis.com/) is the best free stock research website you'll find.

It has financial information, statistics, and valuation metrics on over 100,000 stocks and funds. Plus, the streamlined, user-friendly interface is easy to navigate and the site is _really fast_.

[![Image 4: Apple Stock Page Stock Analysis](https://img.stockanalysis.com/e34d243a-1db5-46e5-8dd0-3f57ae4eac97.jpg)](https://stockanalysis.com/stocks/aapl/)

To get a bit more granular, there are three different categories of historical financial datasets where Stock Analysis really stands out: financial statements, dividends, and corporate actions.

### Best for historical financial statements

In addition to all our real-time data and news, Stock Analysis provides five years of financial statements for free:

[![Image 5: Apple Income Statement Stock Analysis](https://img.stockanalysis.com/5f5ea850-4c57-409b-8be6-1153cbf092e2.jpg)](https://stockanalysis.com/stocks/aapl/financials/)

> **Best-in-class data**
> 
> 
> A lot of finance websites save money by getting their data from second-tier providers, but not us. We wanted a website that was connected to the highest-quality data sources, so we built it ourselves.
> 
> 
> We pay a premium price for these connections, but we think it's worth it.

Additionally, you can unlock up to 30 years of financial statements by [upgrading to Pro](https://stockanalysis.com/pro/) for $9.99/month or $79/year. Pro also comes with unlimited exports (Excel and CSV files), analyst ratings data, unlimited watchlists, and more.

### Best for business metrics

In addition to their financial statements, every company reports specific business metrics that provide valuable insights into their growth, performance, and progress on key objectives.

For instance, Nvidia reports on [Data Center](https://stockanalysis.com/stocks/nvda/metrics/data-center-revenue/) revenue growth, Tesla posts numbers on [deliveries](https://stockanalysis.com/stocks/tsla/metrics/) across each of its models, and Apple reports on [iPhone sales](https://stockanalysis.com/stocks/aapl/metrics/):

[![Image 6: Apple Business Metrics](https://img.stockanalysis.com/e5742f0a-b818-4e5e-9ae4-02b2e0043d13.png.jpg)](https://stockanalysis.com/stocks/aapl/metrics/)

You won't find this level of granularity on an income statement or balance sheet, but it's critical information to know as an investor in these stocks.

Previously, the only way to find these numbers was by digging through each company's quarterly reports on their investor relations websites (which still isn't a bad place to look — more on that below).

But now, on Stock Analysis, you can view company-specific performance metrics for many of the most popular U.S. companies (with more being added all the time), all on one website.

You can access up to 8 quarters or 3 years (TTM) of data completely free.

To unlock the complete history and the ability to export and download these metrics, you'll need a[Pro account](https://stockanalysis.com/pro/).

### Best for dividend history

For yield-focused investors, we provide 10 years' worth of dividend history and charts for free.

[![Image 7: Apple Dividend Info Stock Analysis](https://img.stockanalysis.com/b7f4d532-bda3-47fe-80af-2c0b299379e8.jpg)](https://stockanalysis.com/stocks/aapl/dividend/)

With a [Pro account](https://stockanalysis.com/pro/), you can unlock a stock's full dividend history and export the corresponding charts and data.

### Best for corporate action history

We've also compiled a complete set of corporate actions going back to 1998. You can filter actions by type of action (listed, delisted, splits, spinoffs, etc.) or by year.

[![Image 8: Corporate Actions Stock Analysis](https://img.stockanalysis.com/69dda2cc-6e58-4c87-8362-a4b08d441de9.jpg)](https://stockanalysis.com/actions/)

### Best for historical open/close prices

When I first wrote this article, [Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/) allowed you to download a stock's entire price history for free. You could export each stock's open & close and high & low prices going all the way back to the company's IPO.

While you can still view all of this information for free, you can no longer export this data without being on their $49.95/month Gold plan.

So we (at Stock Analysis) made our own version. Just like on Yahoo Finance, you can view any stock's [historical price data](https://stockanalysis.com/stocks/aapl/history/) for free.

And while we can't allow free exports (our data provider requires us to paywall exports), our plans are much cheaper than Yahoo Finance's (you can become a [Pro subscriber](https://stockanalysis.com/pro/) for just $9.99/month).

### More features

Stock Analysis also has many other useful features available for free, including a [stock screener](https://stockanalysis.com/stocks/screener/)and market [gainers](https://stockanalysis.com/markets/gainers/) and [losers](https://stockanalysis.com/markets/losers/).

2. Koyfin: best for visualizing data and statistics
---------------------------------------------------

[Koyfin](https://stockanalysis.com/out/koyfin) provides access to a wide range of historical financial data, including financial statements, key valuation ratios, and other financial metrics for thousands of stocks.

Instead of only displaying this information in tables, Koyfin allows you to build custom line charts so you can visualize these metrics over time.

This way, you can instantly see how a business is trending.

[![Image 9: Koyfin Apple Stock Page](https://img.stockanalysis.com/d7cf0c0b-5ab6-4223-a8a6-258b52b9cd7b.jpg)](https://stockanalysis.com/out/koyfin)

On Koyfin, you can also compare multiple stocks at once, build customizable dashboards, and create custom templates for quickly analyzing stocks.

You can share charts and analyses with other investors in a few clicks, too.

However, while you can get some of this functionality for free, you'll quickly bump into a paywall if you start using the product regularly. It's $49/month or $468/year for their “Plus” plan, or $110/month or $948/year for their most popular “Pro” plan.

You can try it for free with a [7-day trial](https://stockanalysis.com/out/koyfin).

> Koyfin also ranks #6 on our list of [best stock research websites](https://stockanalysis.com/article/stock-research-websites/).

3. EDGAR (SEC): best for granular research and direct access to 10-Ks
---------------------------------------------------------------------

Every publicly traded company in the United States is required to file a 10-K form (annual, audited financial statement) and 10-Q forms (quarterly, unaudited financial statements) with the SEC.

The SEC makes all of these reports available to the public via its Electronic Data Gathering, Analysis and Retrieval database site, or [EDGAR](https://www.sec.gov/edgar/searchedgar/companysearch) for short.

You can look up any U.S.-listed ticker on the EDGAR website and see its complete filing history.

[![Image 10: Edgar Page Apple](https://img.stockanalysis.com/e7ea4aea-2ffd-42d5-b0af-b9e853761b25.jpg)](https://www.sec.gov/edgar/browse/?CIK=0000320193)

While reading through 10-Ks can be boring, they do contain a lot of granular information that may be hard to find anywhere else.

For instance, if you're trying to find the difference in Apple's gross margin between its products and services or how much of its revenue comes from China, you can hunt down this information in these reports.

They also tend to disclose key business risks to be mindful of.

4. Investor relations pages: best for company-published reports
---------------------------------------------------------------

Most public companies in the U.S. also publish their annual and quarterly reports on their own websites, usually on a page titled “Investor Relations” (e.g., see [Apple's Investor Relations page](https://investor.apple.com/investor-relations/default.aspx)).

[![Image 11: Apple 10 K Page Screenshot](https://img.stockanalysis.com/7b7f21dd-8e81-415c-9ff6-4b285bd219d8.jpg)](https://investor.apple.com/investor-relations/default.aspx)

These reports often contain much of the same information as the 10-Ks they send to the SEC.

However, the financial information is typically summarized, the company's key metrics are made more prominent, and many companies also issue a press release that summarizes the key details.

Additionally, many companies also turn their reports into PDF documents or slide decks (e.g., see [PayPal's Q3 FY24 Investor Update](https://s201.q4cdn.com/231198771/files/doc_financials/2024/q3/PYPL-3Q-24-Earnings-Presentation.pdf)).

These are much easier to read than a text-only 10-K and often contain extra information about what the company's management team is focusing on and what they're forecasting for future quarters.

5. EODHD, Polygon, Marketstack: best historical market data APIs
----------------------------------------------------------------

If you're looking to pull large amounts of data straight into a script or spreadsheet, you're going to need access to an API.

Based on their reliability, broad coverage, and pricing, my three favorites are EODHD, Polygon, and Marketstack.

### EODHD: most complete all‑in‑one package

*   **Coverage:** Equities in 70+ exchanges, ETFs, mutual funds, options, bonds, FX, crypto — plus splits, dividends, fundamentals, and economic indicators.
*   **Data depth:** Daily history back to 1970 for most U.S. tickers and 20+ years for fundamentals.

**Why we like it:** You can get price and fundamentals data with one subscription. [Tiered plans](https://eodhd.com/pricing) start at $19.99/month with a 100,000 daily call limit, so you can prototype cheaply and scale later.

### Polygon: fastest real‑time U.S. equities & options feed

*   **Coverage:** Every U.S. equity and option, plus crypto and forex via REST and WebSocket endpoints.
*   **Data depth:**Tick‑level trades and quotes back to 2004, minute aggregates to 1998, and daily bars to 1980 on most U.S. tickers.

**Why we like it:** Sub‑millisecond latency makes it ideal for intraday dashboards and backtests; the free tier handles end‑of‑day pulls, and [paid plans](https://polygon.io/pricing) (from $99/month) unlock real‑time plus unlimited WebSockets.

### Marketstack: best budget pick for global EOD prices

*   **Coverage:** 170,000+ tickers across 70 exchanges worldwide, including many small‑cap venues that bigger APIs skip.
*   **Data depth:** Daily OHLC back to 2000 for most listings, with intraday intervals (1 min–1 hr) on paid tiers.

**Why we like it:** Clean JSON responses, an Excel/Google Sheets add‑in for no‑code workflows, and a generous free tier (1,000 calls/month). [Paid plans](https://marketstack.com/pricing) start at $9.99/month and scale up to 1 million calls without breaking the bank.

> Whichever you choose, all three offer free tiers or trial credits, so you can test them without spending any money.

Why is historical financial information useful for investors?
-------------------------------------------------------------

It's very easy to get sucked into the day-to-day fluctuations in the stock market and the latest headlines about what's “moving the markets” today.

However, long-term investors know this tends to lead to overtrading and other adverse behavior.

Instead, it's useful to take a step back, zoom out, and look at the larger trends going on within companies. The best way to do this is by looking at historical financial data and statistics to gauge how a stock is trading relative to its own history or industry.

By examining a company's trends in revenue, profit, and other metrics, long-term investors can make more informed, objective, and unemotional investment decisions.

> _Disclaimer: Past performance does not guarantee future results._

Final verdict
-------------

In summary, the best website for you will depend on what type of historical financial data you're looking for, as well as whether you're willing to pay for it.

At [Stock Analysis](https://stockanalysis.com/), we provide five years' worth of financial statements and 10 years of dividend data, plus many other features, for free. To access up to 30 years of financial data and export any data, you'll need to[go Pro](https://stockanalysis.com/pro/) for $9.99/month or $79/year.

Koyfin is a great tool for visualizing a company's financial performance, stock ratios, and other metrics over time. You can try it with a [7-day free trial](https://stockanalysis.com/out/koyfin).

The SEC's [EDGAR](https://www.sec.gov/edgar/searchedgar/companysearch) database, as well as companies' investor relations pages, will give you direct access to 10-Ks and are great resources for digging deep into a company.

For programmers [EODHD](https://eodhd.com/), [Polygon](https://polygon.io/), and [Marketstack](https://marketstack.com/) are excellent API options.

These are my go-to resources for historical stock market data.

# 10 Best Financial Data Providers & Companies

The right financial data provider is crucial for accurate insights and informed decision-making. Here are the five best providers offering reliable, customizable solutions to meet diverse market needs.

## Bright Data

![]()

[**Bright Data**](https://brightdata.com/) offers powerful, customizable financial datasets that help businesses and investors make smarter decisions. Trusted by over 20,000 customers worldwide, Bright Data excels in delivering accurate and validated financial data points like stock prices, volume traded, EPS, and more.
